I agree that while the popularity of our Parks increases and so does our footprint within these Parks, their maintenance is vital to keep them as they were before man even set foot within them. But to make it financially impossible for those who struggle day to day just have the basic necessities to survive on this home we call Earth and visit our most beautiful, protected lands and demand they pay a ridiculous cost is wrong and flat out unAmerican. If the impact of too many people upon these lands is outweighing the cost of their maintenance, then it is time to reduce the number of souls accessing these Parks so they can be enjoyed by all without paying an astronomical price as admission.

"Non sequitur" in logic is an invalid argument where the conclusion could be either true or false (because there is a disconnect between the premises and the conclusion), but the argument nonetheless asserts the conclusion to be true and is thus fallacious.

"Your point is irrelevant whether it stands or not." is clearly not a non sequitur, it is an assertion that without regard to the poll numbers,  the President has made a decision.

You do realize that a 20% downpayment on a $300,000 home means you have $60,000 in the bank.  

100k a year, after taxes works out to something like 5k a month.
Based on your earlier post of 1500 a month in mortgage.
1500 for rent
150 for car insurance
650 for student loans (yes that is about what many of my co-workers were paying monthly)
300 for food
200 for health insurance
500 for 401k(this is assuming that like most employers, they match 50% up to 3%, so stupid not to do this early in your career, personally, I would go higher, but 6% is a good starting point)

This adds up to $3300, leaving you with $1700 or so a month to save towards a downpayment, that works out to about 3 years if nothing goes wrong.  I didn't include anything in here for things like getting sick, a car payment, or other unexpected expenses, $300 a month for food is a bit on the high side, and yes, having an extra $1700 a month seems like a lot, but not if you need to come up with 60k.

One of the ways that people on the bottom of the economic ladder are held down there is unstable housing. When they are forced out of the place they are renting, they often illegally lose their security deposits (tough to find the time to fight for a few hundred bucks when you're working two jobs at low wages). Then they lose all those application fees as they frantically search for new housing. Let's not forget utility deposits and time off work to deal with finding a place to live and moving, plus the moving expense and new commuting costs.

I've owned rental property. I've had tenants absolutely trash some of the houses I have rented. While unfortunate, it's a predictable cost of doing business. I've often taken less than market rates just to keep good tenants in place in order to avoid the bad tenant lottery. I know other landlords who do the same. Unfortunately, some property managers see application fees and deposits as ATM's. Their greed brought this on.
